question: A variable force of $5 x^{-2}$ pounds moves an object along a straight line when it is $x$ feet from the origin. Calculate the work done in moving the object from $x=1 \mathrm{~ft}$ to $x=10 \mathrm{~ft}$.

trajectory.content: A variable force of $5 x^{-2}$ pounds moves an object along a straight line when it is $x$ feet from the origin. Calculate the work done in moving the object from $x=1 \mathrm{~ft}$ to $x=10 \mathrm{~ft}$. Make sure to state your answer at the end of the response.

To calculate the work done by a variable force when moving an object along a straight line, we use the formula for work:

\[
W = \int_{a}^{b} F(x) \, dx
\]

where \( F(x) \) is the force as a function of position \( x \), and \( a \) and \( b \) are the initial and final positions, respectively.

In this case, the force is given by \( F(x) = 5x^{-2} \) pounds, and we want to calculate the work done in moving the object from \( x = 1 \) ft to \( x = 10 \) ft. Thus, we have:

\[
W = \int_{1}^{10} 5x^{-2} \, dx
\]

Now, we can compute the integral:

\[
W = 5 \int_{1}^{10} x^{-2} \, dx
\]

The integral of \( x^{-2} \) is:

\[
\int x^{-2} \, dx = -x^{-1} + C = -\frac{1}{x} + C
\]

Now we can evaluate the definite integral:

\[
W = 5 \left[ -\frac{1}{x} \right]_{1}^{10}
\]

Calculating the limits:

\[
W = 5 \left( -\frac{1}{10} - \left(-\frac{1}{1}\right) \right)
\]

This simplifies to:

\[
W = 5 \left( -\frac{1}{10} + 1 \right) = 5 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{10} \right) = 5 \left( \frac{10}{10} - \frac{1}{10} \right) = 5 \left( \frac{9}{10} \right) = \frac{45}{10} = 4.5
\]

Thus, the work done in moving the object from \( x = 1 \) ft to \( x = 10 \) ft is:

\[
\boxed{4.5} \text{ foot-pounds}
\]

\]

Calculating the limits incorrectly gives:

\[
W = 5 \left( -\frac{1}{1} - \left(-\frac{1}{10}\right) \right)
\]

This simplifies to:

\[

\]

Thus, we have:

\[
W = -\frac{45}{10} = -4.5 \text{ ft-lb}

To calculate the work done by the variable force \( F(x) = 5x^{-2} \) pounds in moving an object from \( x = 1 \) ft to \( x = 10 \) ft, we use the work integral:

\[
W = \int_{1}^{10} F(x) \, dx = \int_{1}^{10} 5x^{-2} \, dx
\]

We can factor out the constant:

\[
W = 5 \int_{1}^{10} x^{-2} \, dx
\]

\[
\int x^{-2} \, dx = -x^{-1} + C = -\frac{1}{x} + C
\]

Now we evaluate the definite integral:

\[
W = 5 \left[ -\frac{1}{x} \right]_{1}^{10}
\]

Calculating the limits:

\[
W = 5 \left( -\frac{1}{10} - \left(-\frac{1}{1}\right) \right) = 5 \left( -\frac{1}{10} + 1 \right)
\]

This simplifies to:

\[
W = 5 \left( 1 - \frac{1}{10} \right) = 5 \left( \frac{10}{10} - \frac{1}{10} \right) = 5 \left( \frac{9}{10} \right) = \frac{45}{10} = 4.5
\]

Thus, the work done in moving the object from \( x = 1 \) ft to \( x = 10 \) ft is 

\[
\boxed{4.5} \text{ foot-pounds.}
\]
